name: Data Structures and Algorithms
scope: A person with BEGINNER proficiency in Data Structures and Algorithms (0-1 years experience — students, bootcamp graduates, fresh hires) should be able to read a clear, narrow problem statement, recognise which standard primitive applies, and write a small function that produces the correct output. They are comfortable with introductory programming concepts: variables and types, loops, conditionals, arrays/lists, strings, basic hash maps, stacks, and FIFO queues. They can perform simple operations on arrays (sum, min, max, average, count, reverse, linear search), simple operations on strings (length, reverse, count vowels, palindrome check, case conversion, simple split-on-space word count), simple counting / frequency problems using a hash map, and simple math (factorial for small n, nth Fibonacci for small n, prime check, sum of digits). They can apply the two-pointer technique on sorted or otherwise locally monotonic arrays, apply binary search on sorted data and on locally monotonic data such as peak-finding, use a stack to validate balanced nesting where order of operations matters, use a FIFO queue for simple level-by-level processing, and write simple recursion with a single base case. They understand FizzBuzz-style branching and can use a single built-in sort followed by a scan. They know to handle the empty-input edge case explicitly and to return a clear sentinel (0, empty list, null, -1) when there is no match. They write idiomatic code in their primary language and lean on standard library / built-in primitives. They do NOT yet design hash-map-backed LRU caches with recency tracking, do not implement graph algorithms (BFS / DFS / shortest path / cycle detection), do not implement dynamic programming, trees / tries, heaps, sliding-window with multiple invariants, or any advanced algorithmic 'tricks'.
long_scope:
{"sections": [{"name": "Basic Array Operations", "scope_included": "- Iterates over a list with a single for / while loop and computes a running aggregate (sum, count, min, max, average)
- Finds the first index of a value via linear search, returning a documented sentinel (-1 / None / null) when not present
- Reverses, filters (even / odd / matches a predicate), and concatenates lists using built-in helpers or a simple loop
- Understands that the language's list / array type supports indexed access in constant time and length in constant time
- Handles empty input and single-element input explicitly", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Basic String Operations", "scope_included": "- Reads a string character by character with a loop or built-in iterator
- Computes string length, reverses a string, counts specific characters (vowels, digits, whitespace)
- Performs case-insensitive comparison by normalising case first
- Checks whether a string is a palindrome under case-insensitive comparison
- Splits a string on whitespace and counts words; trims leading / trailing whitespace before counting
- Knows that string concatenation in a loop can be inefficient and prefers a builder / join helper when appropriate", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Simple Counting and Frequency", "scope_included": "- Uses a hash map (dict / Map / HashMap / map / std::unordered_map) to count occurrences of characters, words, or values
- Finds the most frequent element or any duplicate via a single pass
- Checks whether two strings are anagrams by comparing their frequency maps (or sorted strings)
- Reads from and writes to a hash map idiomatically in the chosen language, including the common get-or-default pattern", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Simple Math on Numbers", "scope_included": "- Computes factorial of a small n (n <= 12) iteratively
- Computes the nth Fibonacci number for small n (n <= 30) iteratively, not via naive recursion
- Checks whether a small positive integer is prime via trial division
- Computes sum of digits, count of digits, and recognises even / odd / divisible patterns
- Understands integer overflow at a basic level for the chosen language's int type", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Simple Sorting and Branching", "scope_included": "- Sorts a list using the language's built-in sort helper, optionally with a key / comparator function
- Takes the top element, last element, or median from a sorted list
- Implements FizzBuzz-style branching with multiple conditions and an else case
- Classifies a numeric value into named bands (low / medium / high) using if-elif chains
- Uses early return for invalid input rather than deeply nested conditionals", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Stacks and Balanced Nesting", "scope_included": "- Uses a stack to validate balanced bracket / parenthesis nesting where the order of opens and closes matters, not just the counts
- Recognises that a counter-based approach silently passes interleaved-but-mismatched inputs like ([)] and reaches for a stack instead
- Handles edge cases: empty input (valid by convention), unmatched closer (invalid), unmatched opener at end (invalid)
- Uses the language's native stack primitive (Python list, JavaScript Array, Java Deque, C++ std::stack, Go slice) idiomatically", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "FIFO Queue Patterns", "scope_included": "- Uses a FIFO queue / deque for simple level-by-level processing where insertion order must be preserved
- Avoids O(n) operations on the wrong end of a list (uses deque / proper queue, not list.pop(0))
- Understands that enqueue and dequeue are constant-time on the language's deque / queue type", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Binary Search on Sorted or Locally Monotonic Data", "scope_included": "- Uses binary search on a sorted array to find an exact match or boundary in O(log n)
- Recognises that binary search also applies to peak-finding and locally monotonic data, not only strictly sorted arrays
- Handles boundary conventions consistently (closed / open ranges, lower vs upper bound)
- Validates assumptions at function entry where sortedness is required, and returns a documented sentinel when no match exists
- Reasons about why O(log n) beats O(n) for large inputs", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Two-Pointer Technique", "scope_included": "- Uses two indices walking from opposite ends (or in the same direction) on a sorted or otherwise monotonic array to find pairs, partitions, or runs
- Recognises when two-pointer can replace a nested O(n^2) scan with a single O(n) pass
- Handles edge cases: empty array, single element, all-equal elements, no valid answer (returns documented sentinel)
- Applies the same two-pointer idea to a singly linked list as slow/fast pointers (finding the middle in one pass, detecting whether the list contains a cycle in O(1) extra space); the ListNode type and any list-construction helpers are always provided in the scaffold — the candidate writes only the traversal logic", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Simple Recursion with One Base Case", "scope_included": "- Writes a recursive function with one clear base case and one recursive step (factorial, sum-of-list, simple divide-and-conquer over an array)
- Reasons about why the recursion terminates and how the call stack unwinds
- Recognises when iterative form is preferable for very large inputs (stack-depth concerns)
- Avoids accidentally exponential branching (naive Fibonacci); prefers iterative or memoised form when the call tree fans out", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Edge Cases and Input Validation", "scope_included": "- Handles empty input and single-element input explicitly with a clearly documented return value
- Handles the no-match case in linear search by returning a sentinel rather than raising
- Recognises and handles negative numbers, zero, and very small inputs where appropriate
- Avoids crashes on inputs at the boundary of the documented constraint range", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Tests for DSA Code", "scope_included": "- Reads the committed test file in the chosen language and understands which behaviours are being asserted
- Adds an assertion for the empty-input case and one boundary case if the committed tests don't already cover them
- Keeps tests deterministic and self-contained (no shared mutable state, no hidden environment dependencies)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Quality, Readability, and Working in a Codebase", "scope_included": "- Writes idiomatic code in the chosen language (Python, JavaScript, Java, C++, or Go) using its standard library / built-in primitives
- Names variables meaningfully (no single-letter loop counters except in trivial loops)
- Returns the documented type from every code path (no implicit None / null leaks)
- Avoids over-engineering: no new class hierarchies, no global state, no unnecessary helpers", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "Out-of-Scope (NOT expected at BEGINNER)", "scope_included": "- LRU caches (hash map + linked list) or any ordered-map / recency-tracking pattern
- Graph algorithms (BFS / DFS / shortest path / topological sort / cycle detection)
- Dynamic programming (1D, 2D, or otherwise)
- Trees, tries, balanced trees, segment trees, suffix structures
- Heaps / priority queues, top-k tracking
- Sliding-window patterns with multiple invariants
- Recursion beyond a single base case (mutual recursion, tree recursion with multiple branches, complex divide-and-conquer)
- Bitwise tricks beyond a basic even / odd check
- Big-O reasoning beyond 'one pass over the input is linear, log-n for binary-search-shaped problems, and constant work per element is O(1)'", "is_non_core_section": true}]}
